text: This video is about the surgical technique to perform robotic ETAP with unilateral ETAR for abdominal wall hernia repair.
The patient is a six-year-old female who has a history of left retroperitoneal approach for aortobiophim bypass.
The video shows a large incisional hernia located in the left lateral abdominal wall and an umbilical hernia in the midline of the patient's abdomen.
A cross-sectional CT scan image displays the incisional hernia located in the patient's left lateral abdominal wall.
The general principles to perform a robotic ETABS, the ports are placed in the retorotospates.
The position of these ports varies depending on the size and location of the hernia and history of previous abdominal surgeries.
The surgeon places the first port, typically the assistant port, on the contralateral side of the lateral abdominal wall hernia.
Three robotic ports are usually required to perform an ETAP procedure.
The surgical image displays the position of the robotic ports on the patient's abdomen, with the patient's head oriented at the top of the screen and feet at the bottom.

The robotic ports are positioned on the right side of the abdomen, extending toward the right retroactive space. The assistant port is located in the right upper abdomen. An arrow indicates the location of the incisional hernia on the left side of the abdomen.
The surgeon enters the retrorectal space and begins dividing the medial contribution of the posterior rectal sheath to the linea alba bilaterally, proceeding from the cephalic to caudal direction.
The surgeon crosses from one retrorectal space to the opposite side, ensuring the peritoneal cavity remains intact.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents and primarily closes defects in the intraabdominal wall. Any peritoneal violations are also closed.

The surgical view shows the right retro-rectal space with the posterior rectal sheath visible at the bottom of the screen, positioned inferiorly. The rectus muscle is seen at the top of the screen, oriented anteriorly, as the camera faces towards the patient's feet.
The surgeon divides the medial contribution of the posterior rectal sheath to the linea alba, continuing the dissection in the retrorectal space.
The surgeon continues the dissection by crossing over to the contralateral side, dividing the attachment of the posterior rectal sheath to the linea alba.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, identifying it as an umbilical hernia.
The surgeon clearly identifies the edges of the defects in the intraabdominal wall, preparing them for proper repair.

The surgeon continues to dissect the midline structures, aiming to create a unified retro-rectal space on both sides. The posterior rectal sheath and rectus muscle are visible, with the surgical field oriented towards the patient's feet.
The surgeon closes all peritoneal violations to prevent bowel herniation into the pre-peritoneal space, ensuring the integrity of the surgical repair.
The surgical team prepares to perform an Endoscopic Transabdominal Repair (ETAR) following the dissection and reduction of the umbilical hernia.

The surgeon completes the retrorectus muscle dissection and incises the posterior lamella of the internal oblique muscle just medial to the linea semilunaris. The transverse abdominis muscle fibers are identified and divided.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to enter the space between the cut edge of the muscle and the underlying transversalis fascia and peritoneum, progressing into the lateral retroperitoneal area.
The surgeon divides the fibers of the transverse abdominal muscle, creating a clear separation in the muscle tissue.
The surgeon bluntly dissects the plane between the transverse abdominal muscle and the underlying fascia and peritoneum to perform the ETAR procedure.
The surgeon closes the left lateral abdominal wall defect by suturing the muscle fibers together, visibly approximating the edges of the defect.
The surgeon closes the midline defects by suturing the edges of the rectus muscles together, bringing the muscle fibers from both sides into alignment.
The surgeon unfolds and positions the mesh in the retro-rectal space beneath the rectus muscle and transverse abdominis.
The surgeon allows air to escape from the surgical field, likely indicating decompression or adjustment of the surgical site.
The patient was discharged home next day. She was seen in the clinic three weeks after surgery. She was doing well with no major issues and no evidence of hernia recurrence.

So in conclusion, robotic ETAP is a safe and feasible procedure for the repair of complex abdominal wall hernias with the ability to perform ETAR at the same time for tension-free closure and for non-midline hernias.
